misconceptionSummary: Students divide the whole parts and decimal parts separately rather than converting to an equivalent whole-number division.

visuals.ruleText: Multiply both dividend and divisor by the same power of 10
visuals.subText: This creates an equivalent whole-number division without changing the answer

steps.narration: Dividing a decimal by a decimal can be converted to a simpler whole-number problem. Multiply both the dividend and divisor by the same power of ten. Because both change by the same factor, the quotient is unchanged.

Four point eight divided by zero point six. Multiply both by ten: forty-eight divided by six. Forty-eight divided by six is eight. The answer is eight — the same as for the decimal problem, because scaling both parts equally preserves the ratio.

Check: zero point six times eight is four point eight — correct. The scaling works because multiplying both numerator and denominator of a division by the same number does not change the value — the ratio is preserved.

visuals.label: Scale both by ×10 — remove decimals, same answer

steps.narration: Eight. Multiply both dividend and divisor by ten to clear the decimals, then perform a straightforward whole-number division.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: dividing 4 by 0 and .8 by .6 separately. Treat the whole division as one expression — multiply both values by 10: 4.8 ÷ 0.6 = 48 ÷ 6 = 8.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Treat a decimal division as a whole. Multiply both parts by the same power of ten to eliminate decimals — the answer is unchanged because the ratio of dividend to divisor stays the same.
